summary: electron-orbit ships hand-rolled TLS exfil client behind SHA256-gated activator and impersonates orbitdb-archive
details: Package self-describes as an Electron-style runtime/icon-renderer toolkit but ships a full hand-rolled TLS 1.3 client (dist/aetherls.js, dist/aead.js, dist/hkdf.js, dist/buf.js implementing ECDHE-P256, AES-128-GCM, HKDF-Expand-Label, ClientHello/Finished, SNI/ALPN) that opens a raw TCP socket via 'node:net' and writes a GET request to an attacker-chosen host, deliberately bypassing Node's https/tls stack to evade proxy interception and certificate pinning. The 'node:net' module name and the 'GET ' request prefix are hidden behind an XOR string decoder (__s(key,data)). The compiled main entry (dist/index.js) contains a SHA256-preimage gate of the form `"0ceaa39...748295".includes(crypto.createHash("sha256").update(process.env.BuildType).digest("hex"))` that flips the package into an alternate branch wiring the TLS retrieval function to a URL placeholder — a covert activator keyed on an environment variable known only to the operator. The identical XOR decoder appears as dead code inside install.js (the postinstall script) indicating a shared obfuscation toolkit across lifecycle and runtime modules. Package name borrows the Electron brand and metadata impersonates the real orbitdb-archive GitHub organization (author 'OrbitDB Archive', homepage https://github.com/orbitdb-archive/orbit-electron) to encourage installation without scrutiny. The combination of cover-story purpose, brand impersonation, custom TLS stack engineered to defeat monitoring, XOR-hidden module/host strings, and an env-var-gated activation switch is the canonical shape of a staged backdoor / covert exfil channel.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
